text.title: Test Structure: AAA and Given-When-Then
text.summary: Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) and Given-When-Then (BDD) are the two primary structural patterns for tests. AAA separates setup, execution, and verification; GWT aligns tests with business language.

rule.statement: Structure every test into exactly three sections separated by blank lines: Arrange (setup), Act (single call), Assert (verify). The Act section must be a single statement.
rule.rationale: A multi-statement Act means you are testing multiple behaviors; split into separate tests. The blank-line separation makes it immediately clear which part of a failing test is at fault.

section.title: Given-When-Then (BDD)

rule.statement: Use Given-When-Then naming and comments for tests that map directly to business requirements or acceptance criteria; it is the same structure as AAA with business-readable labels.
rule.rationale: GWT makes tests readable to non-engineers and serves as executable documentation. The mapping is direct: Given=Arrange, When=Act, Then=Assert.

section.note: GWT and AAA are equivalent in structure. Use GWT when the test name and comments will be read by product owners or QA; use AAA for pure unit tests with no business language requirement.
